section.title: A Bride for Isaac
section.sectionRationale: The longest chapter in Genesis is given to a marriage, because the promise must pass to the next generation. An aged Abraham sends his servant to find a wife for Isaac, and the whole account glows with prayerful trust and God's quiet, faithful guidance.
section.thesis: God faithfully guides the search for Isaac's bride in answer to prayer, carrying the promise forward through Rebekah's willing, God directed yes, so that the covenant line continues by grace and not by chance.
section.frameNote: Six scenes: the oath; the servant's prayer at the well; Rebekah answers the prayer; the errand told to Laban's household; the family's consent; Rebekah becomes Isaac's wife.

verses.text: By now Abraham was old and well along in years, and the LORD had blessed him in every way.
So Abraham instructed the chief servant of his household, who managed all he owned, “Place your hand under my thigh,
and I will have you swear by the LORD, the God of heaven and the God of earth, that you will not take a wife for my son from the daughters of the Canaanites among whom I am dwelling,
but will go to my country and my kindred to take a wife for my son Isaac.”
The servant asked him, “What if the woman is unwilling to follow me to this land? Shall I then take your son back to the land from which you came?”
Abraham replied, “Make sure that you do not take my son back there.

The LORD, the God of heaven, who brought me from my father’s house and my native land, who spoke to me and promised me on oath, saying, ‘To your offspring I will give this land’, He will send His angel before you so that you can take a wife for my son from there.
And if the woman is unwilling to follow you, then you are released from this oath of mine. Only do not take my son back there.”
So the servant placed his hand under the thigh of his master Abraham and swore an oath to him concerning this matter.

units.label: The servant's prayer at the well

verses.text: Then the servant took ten of his master’s camels and departed with all manner of good things from his master in hand. And he set out for Nahor’s hometown in Aram-naharaim.
As evening approached, he made the camels kneel down near the well outside the town at the time when the women went out to draw water.
“O LORD, God of my master Abraham,” he prayed, “please grant me success today, and show {{chesed|kindness}} to my master Abraham.
Here I am, standing beside the spring, and the daughters of the townspeople are coming out to draw water.

Now may it happen that the girl to whom I say, ‘Please let down your jar that I may drink,’ and who responds, ‘Drink, and I will water your camels as well’, let her be the one You have {{appointed|appointed}} for Your servant Isaac. By this I will know that You have shown kindness to my master.”
Before the servant had finished praying, Rebekah came out with her jar on her shoulder. She was the daughter of Bethuel son of Milcah, the wife of Abraham’s brother Nahor.
Now the girl was very beautiful, a virgin who had not had relations with any man. She went down to the spring, filled her jar, and came up again.
So the servant ran to meet her and said, “Please let me have a little water from your jar.”
“Drink, my lord,” she replied, and she quickly lowered her jar to her hands and gave him a drink.
After she had given him a drink, she said, “I will also draw water for your camels, until they have had enough to drink.”
And she quickly emptied her jar into the trough and ran back to the well to draw water, until she had drawn water for all his camels.
Meanwhile, the man watched her silently to see whether or not the LORD had made his journey a success.
And after the camels had finished drinking, he took out a gold ring weighing a beka, and two gold bracelets for her wrists weighing ten shekels.
“Whose daughter are you?” he asked. “Please tell me, is there room in your father’s house for us to spend the night?”
She replied, “I am the daughter of Bethuel, the son that Milcah bore to Nahor.”
Then she added, “We have plenty of straw and feed, as well as a place for you to spend the night.”
Then the man bowed down and worshiped the LORD,

saying, “Blessed be the LORD, the God of my master Abraham, who has not withheld His kindness and faithfulness from my master. As for me, the LORD has led me on the journey to the house of my master’s relatives.”

units.label: The errand told to Laban's household

verses.text: The girl ran and told her mother’s household about these things.
Now Rebekah had a brother named Laban, and he rushed out to the man at the spring.
As soon as he saw the ring, and the bracelets on his sister’s wrists, and heard Rebekah’s words, “The man said this to me,” he went and found the man standing by the camels near the spring.
“Come, you who are blessed by the LORD,” said Laban. “Why are you standing out here? I have prepared the house and a place for the camels.”
So the man came to the house, and the camels were unloaded. Straw and feed were brought to the camels, and water to wash his feet and the feet of his companions.
Then a meal was set before the man, but he said, “I will not eat until I have told you what I came to say.” So Laban said, “Please speak.”
“I am Abraham’s servant,” he replied.
“The LORD has greatly blessed my master, and he has become rich. He has given him sheep and cattle, silver and gold, menservants and maidservants, camels and donkeys.
My master’s wife Sarah has borne him a son in her old age, and my master has given him everything he owns.
My master made me swear an oath and said, ‘You shall not take a wife for my son from the daughters of the Canaanites in whose land I dwell,
but you shall go to my father’s house and to my kindred to take a wife for my son.’
Then I asked my master, ‘What if the woman will not come back with me?’
And he told me, ‘The LORD, before whom I have walked, will send His angel with you and make your journey a success, so that you may take a wife for my son from my kindred and from my father’s house.
And when you go to my kindred, if they refuse to give her to you, then you will be released from my oath.’
So when I came to the spring today, I prayed: O LORD, God of my master Abraham, if only You would make my journey a success!
Here I am, standing beside this spring. Now if a maiden comes out to draw water and I say to her, ‘Please let me drink a little water from your jar,’
and she replies, ‘Drink, and I will draw water for your camels as well,’ may she be the woman the LORD has appointed for my master’s son.
And before I had finished praying in my heart, there was Rebekah coming out with her jar on her shoulder, and she went down to the spring and drew water. So I said to her, ‘Please give me a drink.’
She quickly lowered her jar from her shoulder and said, ‘Drink, and I will water your camels as well.’ So I drank, and she also watered the camels.
Then I asked her, ‘Whose daughter are you?’ She replied, ‘The daughter of Bethuel son of Nahor, whom Milcah bore to him.’ So I put the ring on her nose and the bracelets on her wrists.
Then I bowed down and worshiped the LORD; and I blessed the LORD, the God of my master Abraham, who led me on the right road to take the granddaughter of my master’s brother for his son.
Now if you will show kindness and faithfulness to my master, tell me; but if not, let me know, so that I may go elsewhere.”
Laban and Bethuel answered, “This is from the LORD; we have no choice in the matter.
Rebekah is here before you. Take her and go, and let her become the wife of your master’s son, just as the LORD has decreed.”
When Abraham’s servant heard their words, he bowed down to the ground before the LORD.
Then he brought out jewels of silver and gold, and articles of clothing, and he gave them to Rebekah. He also gave precious gifts to her brother and her mother.
Then he and the men with him ate and drank and spent the night there. When they got up the next morning, he said, “Send me on my way to my master.”
But her brother and mother said, “Let the girl remain with us ten days or so. After that, she may go.”
But he replied, “Do not delay me, since the LORD has made my journey a success. Send me on my way so that I may go to my master.”
So they said, “We will call the girl and ask her opinion.”
They called Rebekah and asked her, “Will you go with this man?” “I will go,” she replied.
So they sent their sister Rebekah on her way, along with her nurse and Abraham’s servant and his men.
And they blessed Rebekah and said to her, “Our sister, may you become the mother of thousands upon thousands. May your offspring possess the gates of their enemies.”
Then Rebekah and her servant girls got ready, mounted the camels, and followed the man. So the servant took Rebekah and left.
Now Isaac had just returned from Beer-lahai-roi, for he was living in the Negev.
Early in the evening, Isaac went out to the field to meditate, and looking up, he saw the camels approaching.
And when Rebekah looked up and saw Isaac, she got down from her camel
and asked the servant, “Who is that man in the field coming to meet us?” “It is my master,” the servant answered. So she took her veil and covered herself.
Then the servant told Isaac all that he had done.
And Isaac brought her into the tent of his mother Sarah and took Rebekah as his wife. And Isaac loved her and was {{comforted|comforted}} after his mother’s death.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast love, loyal kindness, covenant faithfulness. It is the warm, committed loyalty that keeps its promises and does good to those it is bound to. The servant asks God to show chesed to Abraham, and marvels that God has not withheld His chesed and faithfulness, the very heart of God's covenant character.

appointed.gloss: To decide, designate, or appoint, and so to mark out the right one. The servant asks God to point out the woman He has appointed for Isaac, trusting that God has already chosen her. The word underlines that the marriage is God's arranging, not mere coincidence.

comforted.gloss: To be comforted or consoled, to find relief after grief. After Sarah's death Isaac is comforted through his love for Rebekah. The word closes the chapter on a note of healing, as God's provision of a bride brings solace to a grieving son and secures the promised line.

basic.context: Abraham is old, Sarah has died, and the promise now hangs on Isaac having a wife of the covenant family, not a Canaanite. Abraham sends his trusted servant back to his kindred, the servant prays for clear guidance, and God answers precisely through Rebekah at the well. The key question is how God guides the next step of His promise.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a true story told to show God's faithful, providential guidance in answer to prayer. It is not a rulebook for finding a spouse, but it does model trust, prayer, and attention to God's leading. The question is how divine sovereignty and human responsibility work together as the promise moves forward.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is soaked in chesed, God's steadfast, loyal love, which the servant asks for and then praises. He prays that God will point out the woman He has appointed, yakach, for Isaac. At the end Isaac is comforted, nacham, in his grief through Rebekah, and the covenant line is secured.
basic.typology: Some see a picture here: a father sends his servant to win a bride for his beloved son from a far country, and she comes by faith to a bridegroom she has not yet seen. It gently foreshadows the Father, the Spirit, and the Son who gathers a bride, the church, from among the nations.
basic.christ: Isaac is the son of promise who receives a bride prepared by God's own arranging, a faint sketch of Christ, the true Son, who is given a bride, His people, gathered by the Father's initiative and the Spirit's work. Rebekah's willing yes pictures the faith that answers the call of the gospel.
basic.god: God is the one who guides, providing exactly and quickly what His servant prays for. He keeps His covenant chesed to Abraham, superintending even a long journey and a chance meeting at a well. He carries His promise forward faithfully through ordinary means and willing people.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 12 grounds the promise the marriage protects. Proverbs 3 says trust the LORD and He will direct your paths. Ephesians 5 speaks of Christ and His bride, the church. Romans 8 assures that God works all things together for good.
basic.oneStory: The promise passes safely to the next generation as God provides a bride for Isaac. The covenant line advances, not by luck but by God's guiding hand, carrying forward the family through whom the world's Saviour will come.

context.literary: The longest chapter in Genesis, told with unusual leisure and warmth, built on repetition: the servant's prayer, its fulfilment, and then his retelling of it all. The threefold telling slows the reader down to savour God's guidance and the servant's grateful worship.
context.historical: Marriage within the kinship group was normal and valued in the ancient Near East, and marrying into the Canaanite peoples is deliberately avoided for the sake of the covenant. The customs of betrothal gifts, family negotiation, and the bride's consent all fit the world of the patriarchs.
context.canonical: The chapter protects the purity of the covenant line and moves the promise from Abraham to Isaac to the twins to come. Its imagery of a father securing a bride for his son is later taken up in the New Testament's picture of Christ and the church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show God faithfully guiding the choice of Isaac's wife in answer to prayer, preserving the covenant line through a bride from Abraham's own kindred, and to hold up the servant's prayerful trust as a model of walking with God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The account describes how God led in one particular case; it does not prescribe a fleece or a formula for guidance. The enduring lessons are prayerful dependence, attentiveness to God's providence, and gratitude that worships when prayer is answered.

debates.question: Is the servant's specific request at the well a model for how believers should seek God's guidance today?

views.label: A model of humble, prayerful dependence
views.case: The servant prays specifically, ties his request to God's character and promise, and worships when answered; the posture of trust and gratitude is worth imitating.

views.label: A unique providence, not a technique
views.case: The sign the servant asks for is not a general method; the abiding lesson is dependence on God, not laying out fleeces, and the story stresses God's sovereign leading more than a formula.

debates.bearing: Most read the passage as commending the servant's dependent, worshipful trust while cautioning against turning his particular request into a mechanical technique for discerning God's will.

study.originalLanguages: The heart word of the chapter is chesed, the steadfast, covenant love of God. The servant prays that God will show chesed to Abraham, and then praises God for not withholding His chesed and faithfulness. He asks God to designate, yakach, the appointed woman, trusting the choice is God's. The account ends with nacham, comfort: Isaac, grieving his mother, is consoled through Rebekah, and God's loyal love has quietly carried the promise to the next generation.

typology.type: The father sending a servant to secure a bride for the beloved son
typology.fulfillment: The Father and the Spirit gathering a bride, the church, for Christ the beloved Son, Ephesians 5:25-27.

typology.type: Rebekah's willing yes to a bridegroom not yet seen
typology.fulfillment: The faith that answers the gospel call and loves Christ though it has not seen Him, 1 Peter 1:8.

study.christ: The chapter does not name Christ, but its pattern points toward Him. Isaac is the son of promise who receives a bride by the father's initiative, secured from a distant land through a trusted servant. In the fuller story, the Father sends to gather a bride, the church, for His beloved Son, and the Spirit draws her from among the nations. Rebekah's ready answer, I will go, to a bridegroom she has not yet met pictures the faith that responds to Christ's call and loves Him unseen. The comfort Isaac finds foreshadows the joy of the Bridegroom and His people.
study.god: God is the unseen but decisive actor. He hears the servant's prayer and answers before it is finished, arranging a meeting at a well with exactness that draws the servant to his knees in worship. He keeps His steadfast love to Abraham, guiding the long journey and moving hearts to consent. He carries His promise forward through prayer, providence, and willing obedience.

crossReferences.note: The promise the marriage safeguards, that Abraham's line will bless all nations.
Trust in the LORD and He will make your paths straight, embodied in the servant's prayerful guidance.
Christ loves the church as a bridegroom his bride, echoing the pattern of a bride secured for the beloved son.
God works all things together for good, seen in His detailed guidance of the servant's errand.

study.oneStory: With Sarah dead and Abraham old, the promise needs a wife for Isaac, and God provides her, guiding a servant's prayer to Rebekah at the well. The covenant line passes safely to the next generation, not by chance but by God's steadfast love, keeping the family intact through whom the Saviour of the world will come.

quiz.q: Why did Abraham insist that Isaac's wife come from his own kindred rather than the Canaanites?

options.text: To keep the covenant line distinct and faithful to the God of the promise.
options.note: Abraham guards the purity of the line through whom God's promise would come.

options.text: Because he preferred his own relatives over strangers.
options.note: Family ties matter, but the driving concern is the covenant, not mere preference.

options.text: Because the Canaanites were too poor to marry into his family.
options.note: Wealth is not the issue; the concern is faithfulness to God's promise.

options.text: Because God had forbidden Isaac to marry at all.
options.note: The whole chapter is about securing a wife for Isaac, not forbidding marriage.

quiz.q: How was the servant's prayer at the well answered?

options.text: Before he had finished praying, Rebekah came and did exactly what he had asked for as a sign.
options.note: God answers with striking precision and speed in verses 15 to 20.

options.text: After many days of searching, he finally found a willing woman.
options.note: The point is the swift, precise answer, not a long search.

options.text: An angel appeared and named the woman to him.
options.note: Abraham spoke of God's angel going before, but the answer comes through Rebekah at the well, not a naming vision.

options.text: Laban chose a bride and sent her out to him.
options.note: Rebekah is identified before Laban appears; God, not Laban, provides the answer.

quiz.q: What did the servant do when he saw his prayer answered?

options.text: He bowed down and worshiped the LORD, blessing Him for His steadfast love.
options.note: The servant responds to guidance with grateful worship in verses 26 to 27.

options.text: He immediately demanded that Rebekah leave with him.
options.note: He does seek to complete the errand, but his first response is worship.

options.text: He kept the answer secret from Rebekah's family.
options.note: He openly recounts the whole story to the household.

options.text: He doubted whether God had really guided him.
options.note: He is sure God has led him and worships in response.

quiz.q: How does this chapter quietly point toward Christ and the church?

options.text: A father secures a bride for his beloved son through a servant, as the Father gathers a bride for Christ.
options.note: The pattern foreshadows Christ and His church gathered by the Father and Spirit.

options.text: It teaches the exact steps every believer must follow to find a spouse.
options.note: It models trust and prayer but is not a formula; the deeper pattern points to Christ.

options.text: It shows that marriage has no spiritual significance.
options.note: The opposite; the marriage carries the covenant promise and pictures Christ and the church.

options.text: It proves that God leaves such choices entirely to chance.
options.note: God guides in detail; nothing here is left to chance.

quiz.q: What does the chapter reveal about how God guides His people?

options.text: God faithfully leads through prayer and providence, often through ordinary means and willing hearts.
options.note: God answers prayer and arranges circumstances to carry His promise forward.

options.text: God only guides through dramatic miracles and visions.
options.note: God's angel is mentioned, but the guidance comes through prayer and providence at a well, not spectacle.

options.text: God guides only those who never face uncertainty.
options.note: The servant faces real uncertainty and prays through it; guidance meets him there.

options.text: God leaves His promises to succeed or fail by human effort alone.
options.note: God Himself secures the outcome; human effort serves His guiding hand.

questions: The servant prayed specifically and then watched for God's answer. Where do you need to bring a specific request to God and trust Him with the outcome?
When his prayer was answered, the servant stopped to worship. How well do you pause to thank God when He answers, rather than rushing on to the next thing?
Rebekah said simply, I will go, to a future she could not fully see. Where is God asking you to say yes before every detail is clear?
God guided a long journey and a meeting at a well to keep His promise on track. How does this steady you when your own path feels uncertain?
Isaac was comforted in his grief through God's provision of Rebekah. How have you seen God bring comfort through the people He has placed in your life?

translationNotes.note: Placing the hand under the thigh was a solemn oath gesture, likely tied to the covenant sign, underscoring the weight of the servant's sworn commission.
The BSB here contains a dash setting off the promise quotation; it has been rendered as a comma so the sentence reads, To your offspring I will give this land, He will send His angel before you.
The Hebrew verb rendered meditate is uncommon and can also mean to walk about or to muse; the sense is of Isaac out alone in reflective quiet as the caravan approaches.
